visual_dna.age_range: ~30-40 — likely the older sister, head of the household
visual_dna.skin_tone: warm olive, slightly flushed — she is always in motion
visual_dna.hair_color: dark brown, partially covered but often disheveled from work
visual_dna.hair_texture: straight to wavy — usually under a working head covering
visual_dna.eye_color: dark brown — direct, capable, frustrated in the key scene
visual_dna.height_build: medium, capable and strong — someone who runs a household
distinguishing_marks: always moving — hands busy, eyes scanning what needs doing
she is probably the homeowner — Jesus is welcomed into 'her' home (Luke 10:38)
the frustration when Mary sits down is genuine and very human
she is not wrong to serve — she is wrong to resent

wardrobe_by_arc.hosting_jesus: home working clothes — practical, slightly disheveled from cooking and preparing, apron equivalent

voice_profile.register: warm alto, slightly clipped when stressed — the voice of someone used to managing
voice_profile.pace: fast — she talks like someone who has a list
voice_profile.accent_note: Judean Aramaic — Bethany is near Jerusalem
speech_mannerisms: 'Lord, don't you care that my sister has left me to do all the work by myself?' — a complaint addressed to Jesus, not Mary
she escalates: she asks Jesus to take her side
the rebuke she receives is the most gentle in the gospels: 'Martha, Martha...' — repeated name, full of tenderness
voice_profile.notes: Martha is not a villain. She is doing an excellent thing — hospitality is sacred. Jesus doesn't say she's wrong to serve. He says she's anxious about too many things. The distinction matters enormously for portrayal.

emotional_arc.opening: She welcomes Jesus into her home — a woman of genuine faith and hospitality.
emotional_arc.midpoint: She is overwhelmed with preparations — and looks up to find her sister sitting at Jesus's feet learning.
emotional_arc.climax: 'Martha, Martha, you are worried and upset about many things, but few things are needed — or indeed only one.'
emotional_arc.resolution: Luke ends the scene there. In John, her faith statement 'I believe that you are the Messiah' is one of the strongest confessions in any gospel — she is no small figure.

evangelical_baptist.notes: The lesson about choosing the better thing — Martha vs Mary as teaching on priorities
evangelical_baptist.marketing_angle: She was doing everything right. And still missed the moment. We know this feeling.

catholic.notes: St. Martha — patron of hospitality, domestic workers. Her John 11 faith statement is deeply resonant.
catholic.marketing_angle: St. Martha — the patron of those who serve. Her frustration is the most relatable in the New Testament.

black_church.notes: The church mother who does everything — the one who carries the load and sometimes resents it
black_church.marketing_angle: She did everything. She cooked, she served, she welcomed. And she was exhausted. We see you.

hispanic_christian.notes: The woman who prepares the meal — the one who makes everything happen. Very resonant.
hispanic_christian.marketing_angle: She's the one who made it happen. The one who cooked while others listened. The most relatable woman in the gospel.

secular_curious.notes: The most human character in the scene — she breaks social convention to complain directly to Jesus
secular_curious.marketing_angle: She complained to Jesus directly. He didn't rebuke her for complaining — he answered the real problem.

youth_gen_z.notes: The overworked one who finally says it out loud
youth_gen_z.marketing_angle: She was the one doing everything and finally said it out loud. And Jesus didn't tell her to be quiet.

jewish_american.notes: The Jewish woman hosting in her home — hospitality as sacred duty, the conflict between duty and presence
jewish_american.marketing_angle: Hospitality is a mitzvah. She was doing it right. Jesus complicated that.

women.notes: She is seen and named — her frustration is valid even if the answer reorients it
women.marketing_angle: She said what every woman carrying everything alone has thought. Jesus answered with tenderness, not dismissal.

men.notes: Supporting character — more resonant for women
men.marketing_angle: He called her by name twice. 'Martha, Martha.' That's not a rebuke. That's love.

audience_targeting.modern_parallel: The person doing all the work who finally asks why no one else is helping — and gets an unexpected answer
audience_targeting.modern_parallel_detail: Her complaint is legitimate. Her resentment is understandable. The answer she receives doesn't dismiss her work — it reframes what the work is for.

ai_generation.consistency_seed_prompt: 1st century Jewish woman age 30-40, warm olive skin, dark hair partially under working head covering, flushed and in motion, practical home working robes, interior of a Judean home, holding a serving bowl or cooking implement, expression of frustration mixed with deep faith, cinematic photoreal, warm interior lamplight, 8k
ai_generation.negative_prompt: cartoonish villain expression, European features, modern clothing, glowing features
ai_generation.midjourney_style_params: cinematic photoreal, warm interior domestic light, active practical energy
ai_generation.firefly_prompt: Photorealistic portrait of a 1st century Jewish woman, age 30-40, olive skin, dark hair under practical head covering, dressed in working home clothes, in the interior of a Judean home with cooking vessels and serving dishes nearby, expression of exhausted frustration on the face of someone who carries everything, warm interior light
reference_films: The Chosen TV series — Martha portrayed as capable homeowner
ai_generation.color_grading_notes: Domestic warmth — oil lamp interior light. The interior should feel like genuine hospitality, which makes her complaint even more poignant.

production_notes: Do not play Martha as petty. She is running a home, welcoming a traveling teacher and his twelve hungry disciples. She is overwhelmed for legitimate reasons. The gentle rebuke is the point, not her fault.
